summary: Gruber responds to the unmasking of the 'Fake Steve Jobs' blogger, revealed by The New York Times to be Forbes editor Daniel Lyons. He argues against two wrong reactions: pretending the anonymity can be preserved, and assuming the blog is now over. Gruber contends that Fake Steve's appeal was always rooted in the quality of the writing — scathingly funny and deeply insightful — not the mystery of the author's identity. He draws an analogy to SNL impressions, where the performance matters more than the secret. He suggests Lyons may actually write with more freedom now that he has nothing to lose.
key_points: Two common reactions to the unmasking are both wrong: trying to preserve the fiction of anonymity, and assuming the blog is finished
Fake Steve's appeal was the quality of the writing — funny, insightful, and cleverly metafictional — not the mystery of authorship
Lyons created a sophisticated narrative voice that simultaneously acknowledges being Fake Steve while never fully breaking character as Real Steve
Losing anonymity could actually liberate Lyons to write with even more ferociousness since he has nothing left to protect
money_quotes: Wishing the balloon had never popped is one thing. Trying to unpop it is another.
Knowing who the author is doesn't spoil Fake Steve any more than knowing which cast member is playing the president on Saturday Night Live. It's the performance that counts, not the secret.
That his identity was unknown certainly added a mystique, but it was nothing more than a distraction from the work itself.
Now that his identity has been revealed, he's got nothing to lose.
He might as well just let it fly.
It's not like he can simply go back to conducting boring-ass interviews with Steve Ballmer for Forbes.
key_insight: Great satirical writing derives its power from the quality of the performance, not the mystery of who's behind it, and unmasking the author may actually free them to be bolder.
